user_request: Draft a Summary Suit under Order XXXVII CPC for recovery of Rs.9,00,000/- based on a dishonoured cheque. Cheque No. [CHEQUE_NUMBER] dated [CHEQUE_DATE] drawn on [DRAWEE_BANK], [BRANCH] Branch, Bengaluru. Cheque was returned unpaid with bank return memo endorsed 'Funds Insufficient' dated [RETURN_DATE]. Statutory legal notice was issued and duly served on the Defendant. Defendant failed to pay despite notice. Claim principal Rs.9,00,000/-, interest at 18% per annum from cheque date till realization, and costs. Draft in Commercial Court format suitable for filing before the Commercial Court at Bengaluru. Include Order XXXVII maintainability paragraph, cause of action, limitation, valuation, court fee and prayer clause. Plead limitation properly.

facts.summary: Drafting a Summary Suit under Order XXXVII CPC for recovery of Rs. 9,00,000/- based on a dishonoured cheque drawn on [DRAWEE_BANK], [BRANCH] Branch, Bengaluru. The cheque was returned unpaid due to insufficient funds, and a statutory legal notice was served without payment.

chronology.event: Cheque returned unpaid with reason 'Funds Insufficient'
Statutory legal notice issued and duly served on Defendant
Defendant failed to pay despite notice

evidence.description: Dishonoured Cheque No. [CHEQUE_NUMBER] drawn on [DRAWEE_BANK], [BRANCH] Branch

evidence.relevance: Proof of demand prior to suit

slots.value: Summary Suit under Order XXXVII CPC

assumptions: Jurisdiction is Bengaluru based on bank branch location
Suit is filed under Commercial Court jurisdiction
Debt is liquidated and based on a written instrument
Suit value falls within pecuniary jurisdiction of Commercial Court

By the amending Act 104 of 1976, Act 127 of the Limitation Act, 1963 was amended providing for 60 days instead 
of 30 days as the period of limitation for filing an application to set aside a sale on execution of a decree, including 
any such application by a judgment debtor. A five judge bench of the Supreme Court, overruling the decision inP K 
Unni v. Nirmala Industries 113 observed
Viewed in this context the intention of the legislature in extending the period under Act 127, Limitation Act may be 
seen. It is very clear from the statement of objects and reasons, which have been set out herein above, that the 
period under art 127, Limitation Act was extended from 30 days to 60 days in order to give more time to persons to 
make deposits. The legislature has noted that the period of 30 days from the date of sale was too short and often 
caused hardships because judgment-debtors usually failed to arrange for moneys within that period.114
The Bench further observed (Variava J, speaking for the court) as follows in para 18 of the judgment:

are no different periods of limitation for making deposits and/or filing an application for setting aside the sale. It is by

plaintiffs counsel to pay the court fee, despite the fact that the same was purchased and handed over to the 
counsel, the plaint can be restored under s 151 and that order will not be open for interference in revision.90 In a

In case where the subject matter is not capable of being estimated at a money value, it is provided by s 9 of the 
Suits Valuation Act, 1887, that the value of the suit for purposes of jurisdiction is what the High Court may 
specify by rules made under that section. Where no rules are made, the High Courts of Allahabad and Calcutta 
have said that the safest and most convenient course is to treat the valuation made by the plaintiff as prima 
facie the true valuation, but subject to correction by the court. If the defective valuation has been due to an 
improper motive, the court must decide what should be considered to be the proper value. 53 The cases in which 
the above rule was laid down were suits for restitution of conjugal rights. In a subsequent case, however, the
